introduction: A rare but potentially serious clinical entity. Occurs in about 1 – 4% of all pregnancies. Incidence and prevalence of all heart disease varies from place to place. Rheumatic heart disease is more commonly found in less affluent societies while congenital heart disease now accounts for approximately 50% of cardiac diseases in pregnancy in the UK.

features: Class 1: No limitation of physical activity
Class 2: Slight to moderate limitation of physical activity: ordinary day-to-day activities cause dyspnoea
Class 3: Marked limitation of activity. Minimal exertion causes dyspnoea
Class 4: Symptoms at rest; unable to carry out any physical activity without dyspnoea; orthopnoea may be present
Epigastric or sub-xiphoid pain; bloating, heartburn
Oedema (legs; occasionally hands and face); Varicose veins
Capillary telangiectasia Prominent jugular venous pulsations
Sinus tachycardia; ectopic beats, Third heart sound
Widely split S and S heart sounds1, Murmurs

investigations: Serial blood cultures (if infective endocarditis is suspected)

non_drug: Fully evaluate patient in conjunction with a cardiologist
Surgically correct any defect that is amenable

Counsel on risk of maternal death, possible reduction of maternal life expectancy, risk of foetus developing congenital heart disease, foetal growth restriction, possibility of pre-term labour, need for frequent hospital attendance, possible admission, need for intensive maternal and foetal monitoring in labour
Extreme vigilance: most features of cardiac failure are present in pregnancy
Watch for and treat respiratory or urinary tract infection aggressively
Monitor for anaemia, obesity, multiple gestations for intensive care
Examine for ankle and sacral oedema, pulse rate and rhythm, blood pressure, jugular venous pressure, basal crepitations, SFH measurement
Competent dental care: full inspection, advise on oral hygiene, dental treatment under antibiotic cover
Elastic stockings or tights to prevent pooling of blood in the veins of lower limb
Termination of pregnancy and sterilization in severe debilitating cases
Foetal surveillance: ultrasound scan for cardiac anomaly at 22 weeks
Either delivery for maternal or foetal indications
Shorten 2 stage with low cavity forceps delivery
Oxytocin for third stage, avoid ergometrine
Oxygen available and used if needed
drug: Heparin safer in pregnancy than warfarin which is teratogenic
Prophylactic antibiotics to prevent bacterial endocarditis
supportive_measures: Elastic stockings or tights to prevent pooling of blood

complications: Maternal mortality in various conditions (25 - 50% in Eisenmenger's syndrome; 5% in tetralogy of Fallot; 1% in rheumatic heart disease)
Congestive cardiac failure: greatest risk post-partum
Foetal complications: Rheumatic heart disease causing intrauterine growth restriction, pre-term delivery, cyanotic congenital heart disease, poor outcomes with foetal loss up to 40%

Uncorrected coarctation of aorta leading to foetal growth restriction, pre-maturity, small for gestation age, intrauterine growth restriction, intrauterine fetal death, 10 - 15% chance of baby having congenital heart disease
prevention: Competent dental care under antibiotic cover
Adequate antenatal, intrapartum and postpartum care
Early detection and management of pregnancy-induced hypertension
other_investigations: Chest radiograph is better avoided in pregnancy
